articles.title: BCCI's Proposed Rule Change Could Transform How Players Retire - Details Inside
articles.description:
The Board of Control for Cricket in India (BCCI) is strongly considering a sweeping policy change that could fundamentally redefine player retirements and their subsequent involvement in overseas T20 franchise leagues, Cricbuzz reported. The cricket board is looking to discourage early retirements, ensuring players do not leave domestic or international cricket solely to join foreign franchise competitions.

During an online Apex Council meeting, BCCI deliberated on a strict proposal that would enforce a minimum five-year cooling-off period before any retired player who has competed in foreign leagues can return to any official role or capacity within Indian cricket.

Closing Retirement "Grey Zone"

Under the current framework, active Indian cricketers - whether international stars, domestic stalwarts, or IPL-contracted personnel - are explicitly barred from participating in rival global T20 leagues. However, a regulatory loophole has allowed players to abruptly announce their retirement from the Indian ecosystem, instantly making themselves eligible to cash in on lucrative opportunities abroad.

Also Read | BCCI Picks New T20I Captain, To Lead India In Ireland And England Tours

"The idea is to send a message to the players to be sure of their decision. Modalities will be worked out before rules are framed, looking at all parameters," said a BCCI official told Hindustan Times.

Under the discussed framework, the cooling-off period would be reduced to just a single year if a retired individual chooses not to play in any league globally during that timeframe. However, those stepping directly onto the foreign franchise circuit would face the full five-year restriction.

After announcing his retirement, Vijay Shankar secured a deal with the Kandy Royals for the 2026 Lanka Premier League. His decision mirrors those of several former Indian cricketers - among them Dinesh Karthik, Yuvraj Singh, Unmukt Chand, Pravin Tambe, and Irfan Pathan - who left domestic cricket behind to participate in overseas franchise tournaments.

articles.title: FIFA 2026: Japan switches Monterrey training sites ahead of World Cup amidst pitch problems
articles.description: Japan's World Cup team has moved its training base in Monterrey. The original site had an uneven pitch. The team will now train at El Barrial. This change comes ahead of their World Cup matches. Japan plays its first game on June 14. The team's main training camp is in Nashville, Tennessee.

articles.title: “I offer my sincere apology”: Kaishu Sano controversy returns as Japan fans question 2026 FIFA World Cup place
articles.description: Kaishu Sano has become a major talking point ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup. The Japan midfielder’s inclusion in the national squad has revived discussion about his 2024 arrest in Tokyo. Although prosecutors later dropped the case after a settlement, fans remain divided. As Japan prepares for the tournament, debate around Sano continues across social media.

articles.title: Serena Williams shares first look at Queen’s Club while long awaited comeback at 44 draws closer
articles.description: Serena Williams has been pictured training at Queen’s Club in London ahead of her return to professional tennis at age 44. The 23-time Grand Slam champion will play doubles with Victoria Mboko at the HSBC Championships next week. Naomi Osaka and Coco Gauff welcomed the comeback, while fans eagerly wait to see Williams compete again.

articles.title: Seth Jarvis nets OT winner as Hurricanes rally to even series against Golden Knights in Stanley Cup Final
articles.description: Carolina Hurricanes staged a stunning comeback, defeating Vegas Golden Knights 4-3 in overtime of Game 2. Seth Jarvis netted the winner on a power play, capping a dramatic turnaround after Vegas held a two-goal lead. Logan Stankoven ignited the rally, followed by Mark Jankowski and Jordan Staal. Mark Stone forced overtime for Vegas, but Carolina ultimately prevailed.
